title: [Security]: /metrics endpoint default-unauthenticated exposes multi-tenant PII in production deployments
body:
## Summary

The `/metrics` Prometheus endpoint is **unauthenticated by default** and exposes sensitive multi-tenant data in production LiteLLM Proxy deployments. While an opt-in authentication mechanism exists (`require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: true`), the insecure default leads to widespread real-world exposure.

This is a follow-up to #13644, which was auto-closed by the stale bot as `NOT_PLANNED` without any maintainer response — despite multiple users confirming the security impact.

## Vulnerability Details

**Type:** Information Disclosure / Tenant Isolation Bypass  
**Default behavior:** `/metrics` endpoint serves ~2MB of Prometheus metrics to any unauthenticated HTTP request  
**CVSS 3.1:** 7.5 (High) — `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N`

## Data Exposed via `litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric` Labels

The Prometheus metrics include per-request labels that leak the following data for **every tenant**:

| Label | Sensitive Data |
|-------|---------------|
| `hashed_api_key` | SHA-256 hash of customer API keys |
| `api_key_alias` | Human-readable key names (often containing company names) |
| `team` | Team UUIDs |
| `team_alias` | Company names and **employee email addresses** (PII) |
| `end_user` | End-user identifiers |
| `user` | User identifiers with email addresses |
| `requester_ip_address` | Client IP addresses revealing infrastructure topology |
| `user_agent` | Full user-agent strings (exposing Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs, automation tools) |
| `model` | AI models accessed per customer |
| `model_id` | Internal model UUIDs |

## Real-World Impact (Verified)

During authorized security testing, we identified production LiteLLM Proxy deployments where the unauthenticated `/metrics` endpoint exposed:

- **15+ customer tenants** with complete operational telemetry
- **Corporate email addresses** in `team_alias` fields (GDPR-relevant PII)
- **Client IP addresses** enabling infrastructure geolocation and cloud provider identification
- **Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs** in user-agent strings, enabling reverse engineering of customer automation infrastructure
- **API key hashes** with aliases mapping to specific companies
- **Cross-tenant request patterns** enabling competitive intelligence gathering

A single unauthenticated GET request returns the complete dataset for all tenants.

## Root Cause

The `/metrics` endpoint is registered without any authentication middleware by default. The existing `require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint` setting is:

1. **Opt-in** rather than opt-out (insecure by default)
2. **Not prominently documented** in deployment guides
3. **Not enabled in the default/example configurations**

This violates the principle of **secure by default** — especially critical for a multi-tenant proxy handling API credentials.

## Recommendation

1. **Make authentication the default** for `/metrics` — require explicit opt-out (`allow_unauthenticated_metrics: true`) rather than opt-in authentication
2. **Sanitize metric labe

title: [Minter] Add solo staking
body:
Users should be able to use our contract for solo staking. 
To start we would require users to have the following: 
- 32 ETH
- 1000 SGT bonded and timelocked in a way that admins can unlock it when the users exit their validators or slash it if the user acts in a nefarious way.   

For this we will need to upgrade the send2eth2 function in the minter to use AccessControl over having a single owner.  

For a simplified implementation, the SGT bonded user should be able to call the function directly, resulting in a deposit function call which returns 32 vETH2 to them in exchange for 32.1 ETH.  

The solo staker can then use their vETH2 to earn profits at a higher APY in our ecosystem while waiting for ETH2 rewards to be unlocked via a chain merge or other mechanism the EF comes up with.  

This way the solo staker holds onto both their private keys for withdrawals and the validator. 

The solo staker will be responsible for running their own validator to start with and entering the correct public keys for the call registering the validator.

title: Bounty: Implement InkCanvas and InkPresenter controls (lifetime commercial license reward)
body:
## Bounty
Whoever fully implements `InkCanvas` + `InkPresenter` parity for this repository will receive a **lifetime commercial license** for InkkSlinger.

## Why this issue is combined
`InkPresenter` is the stroke-rendering primitive hosted by `InkCanvas`. In WPF terms they are tightly coupled. In this repository, implementing them together in one PR avoids partial API surfaces that are hard to validate.

## Target outcome
Deliver a practical MVP parity slice where users can:
- place an `InkCanvas` in XML/XAML,
- draw strokes with pointer input,
- see strokes rendered and clipped correctly,
- keep behavior deterministic with focused regression tests,
- keep full solution + test suite green.

## Repository orientation (start here)
Read these files first:
- `README.md`
- `TODO.md`
- `UI-FOLDER-MAP.md`

### High-value local references for implementation patterns
- Input routing and focus/capture orchestration:
  - `UI/Managers/Root/Services/UiRootInputPipeline.cs`
  - `UI/Input/Core/FocusManager.cs`
- Base control and rendering lifecycle:
  - `UI/Controls/Base/UIElement.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/Control.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/FrameworkElement.cs`
- Pointer-handling control examples:
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/RichTextBox.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Scrolling/ScrollViewer.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/Slider.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
- Rendering helpers:
  - `UI/Rendering/Core/UiDrawing.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Primitives/Shape.cs`
- XAML object creation and property-element plumbing:
  - `UI/Xaml/Core/XamlLoader.cs`
- Schema authoring:
  - `Schemas/InkkSlinger.UI.xsd`
- Recently added reference pattern for introducing a new control end-to-end:
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
  - `InkkSlinger.Tests/DocumentViewer*Tests.cs`

## Suggested implementation architecture
This is a recommended shape, not a strict requirement.

### 1) Core ink model types (new)
Add small, testable model types under `UI/Text/Viewing/` or `UI/Input/` or another coherent location (maintainer discretion):
- `InkStroke`
  - `IReadOnlyList<Vector2>` points
  - stroke color/thickness/opacity
  - optional bounding box cache
- `InkStrokeCollection`
  - add/remove/clear
  - change notifications for invalidation hooks
- optional `InkDrawingAttributes` style object if you want WPF-like configuration

Keep allocations controlled on pointer-move hot paths.

### 2) `InkPresenter` control (new)
`InkPresenter` should be the rendering primitive for stroke collections:
- derive from `Control`
- expose DP(s) for stroke source / stroke visual settings
- render strokes in `OnRender(SpriteBatch)`
- support clipping (`TryGetClipRect`) consistent with surrounding controls
- invalidate minimally when stroke collection mutates

### 3) `InkCanvas` control (new)
`InkCanvas` should host `InkPresenter` and own interaction semantics:
- derive from `Control` (or container base if required)
- expose DP(s) for stroke collection and editing mode
- pointer interaction path:
  - pointe

title: Title: Invalid threshold input (“$ of X”) triggers fallback mode that duplicates entropy and leaks checksum-index hiding mechanism (significant implementation bug)
body:
Hi Bitaps team,
I’m a self-taught BTC learner who has been testing your Mnemonic Secret Sharing tool at [bitaps.com](http://bitaps.com/) (the one linked from the 1 BTC Shamir challenge page). While playing with the “Mnemonic secret sharing schema threshold” form I discovered unexpected behavior that appears to be a significant implementation bug.
Steps to reproduce:
1.  Go to the Mnemonic secret sharing tool.
2.  In the input box, paste any 12-word mnemonic (I tested both my own phrases and the two public challenge shares concatenated as one 24-word string).
3.  Set the threshold to something invalid like $ of 5 or $ of 8 (or any non-numeric value in the first box).
4.  Click “Calculate”.
5.  Repeat “Calculate” several times.
Expected behavior:
The tool should either reject the invalid threshold with a clear error (“threshold must be a number between 1 and 255”) or refuse to generate shares, since proper Shamir requires a valid integer threshold.
Actual behavior (what I observed every time):
•  The tool silently generates the exact number of “shares” I typed in the “of X” box (5, 8, etc.).
•  Every share is identical in the first 11 words (the entropy part is simply duplicated).
•  Only the 12th word (checksum) changes.
•  Repeatedly hitting Calculate makes the 12th word cycle alphabetically through valid BIP39 words (example cycle I captured on multiple runs): funny → fury → furnace → future → gadget → gain → galaxy → gap → garlic → garment → game → garden → garage → garbage → etc.
•  Changing the last word of my input phrase (e.g. to something starting with “d”) shifts the entire cycle to words starting with that letter.
•  The cycle length seems capped around 16 possibilities, which matches the 4 checksum bits described in your BIP/mnemonic-improvement.md.
This happens with any input, including the two public challenge shares you published.
Why this is a bug (and why it matters for the challenge): Your own documentation explains that share indices are hidden in the 4 checksum bits of the last word so every share still looks like a normal BIP39 mnemonic. In normal operation (valid 3-of-5 threshold) the tool should run real GF(256) Shamir polynomials and produce completely different entropy in each share.
When the threshold is invalid, it falls back to this trivial “duplicate + cycle checksum for index” mode. This exposes the index-hiding trick in plain view and shows that input validation is missing (NaN checks are not handled, so the code skips the real sharing logic).
This matches the “significant implementation bug” category in your bug bounty (0.05 BTC+ tier) and could potentially weaken share indistinguishability or allow reconstruction tricks from fewer than the required shares.
Impact:
•  Makes the tool behave unpredictably on bad input.
•  Reveals internal mechanics that should stay hidden.
•  Possibly related to why no one has claimed the main 1 BTC challenge yet (the fallback could be exploitable).
Suggested fix:
•  Add proper type-c

title: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

Implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal/TTNN on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware, which would enable usage of discrete wavelet transforms (DWTs) on device.

## 📝 Background

Discrete wavelet transform will help us enable multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks:
- JPEG2000 lossy compression.
- Real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines.
- Audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data processing applications.

## 🎯 What Success Looks Like

Requirements
- Support 1D/2D inputs with batch dimension (B, 1, H, W).
  - Layout can be both row major and tile. Preferably RM for 1d input, and tile layout for 2d input.
- Support the wavelets from PyWavelets catalog:
  - Haar: db1
  - Daubechies: dbN
  - Biorthogonal (used in JPEG2000): biorN.M
  - Symlets: symN
  - Coiflets: coifN
  - Discrete Meyer: dmey
- Support fp32 (SFPU-based implementation)
- Should be implemented as ttnn operation (`ttnn.dwt`/`ttnn.idwt`)
  - Should be implemented as optimized multicore kernel (not a naive composite version)
- Precision targets for the SFPU fp32 (Wavelet categories are defined in [the table below](#wavelet-categories)):
- Compact lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size <= 12`, `num_steps <= 11`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e2`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.

- Medium lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `12 < tap_size <= 24`, `12 <= num_steps <= 23`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e4`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.

- Large / numerically sensitive lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size > 24`, or `num_steps > 23`, or `max_abs_coeff >= 1e4`.
  - Forward precision is best effort.
  - Reconstruction precision is best effort.
  - These wavelets should report per-wavelet median / p95 / max L2 relative error and known outliers, but should not be treated as hard-gating failures.

- Precision reports should always include a per-wavelet breakdown and explicitly call out known SFPU fp32 outliers.


- No strict requirements for potential lower precision implementation.

- Optionally
  - Investigate FPU implementation (im2col/conv based) with tf32/bf16 + acc fp32 precision
  - Support l1 sharded inputs
  - Support arbitraty number of

title: [Feature Request]: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform and Inverse LWT
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

**By** @draklowell, @ke1rro, @kerfmanner, @sodaqqq *@ Ukrainian Catholic University*

## Summary
We target to implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal to enable O(N) multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks efficient wavelet-based AI architectures (WaveKAN), JPEG2000 compression, and real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines without CPU fallback. The implementation will be memory-aware and optimized for Tensix L1 constraints and NoC topology.

## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

While work on Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is currently in progress within the tt-metal ecosystem ([#21412](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/21412)), FFT alone is insufficient for modern signal processing tasks dealing with **non-stationary signals**. FFT provides frequency information but completely loses temporal localization - it cannot tell _when_ a particular frequency event occurred.

The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) applications are vast and critical for many domains:

- **Advanced Digital Signal Processing**: Processing audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data where _when_ a frequency occurs is as important as _which_ frequency. Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) attempts to solve this but suffers from fixed resolution (Heisenberg uncertainty principle) and higher computational cost.
- **Next-Gen AI Models**: Emerging architectures like **WaveKAN** (Wavelet Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks) replace standard activation functions with wavelet bases to improve convergence, interpretability, and function approximation. Currently, there are no native ops to accelerate these models on Wormhole/Blackhole.
- **Signal Compression**: Wavelet transforms are the foundation of JPEG 2000 and modern audio codecs, offering superior compression without block artifacts compared to DCT-based methods.
- **Denoising**: Wavelet thresholding is one of the most effective techniques for noise removal across domains - from medical imaging to financial time series.

## Describe the solution you'd like

We propose implementing Discrete Wavelet Transform via **LWT** and **ILWT** as a logical extension to the DSP capabilities being built around FFT.

Our team has been exploring the **Lifting Scheme** (Sweldens, 1996) as the implementation approach.

The Lifting Scheme decomposes the transform into three simple steps that are done in-place:
- Split: Separate even/odd indexed samples.
- Predict: Predict odd samples from even (generates detail coefficients).
- Update: Update even samples (generates approximation coefficients).

![Lifting scheme (from Wikipedia)](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b011cc22-0709-4bb9-a8df-8586bc583ab4)

### Why Lifting Scheme?

We believe this is the right fit for Tenstorrent hardware for the following reasons:
| Property | Fast Wavelet Transform | Lifting Wavelet Transform |
|--

title: [Security #5] Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)
body:
---
title: "Security #5: Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "cache", "deserialization"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #5

**Severity:** High  
**Component:** Mint Cache Layer  
**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py` (line 54)  
**Impact:** Potential RCE, data manipulation, cache poisoning  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925 (same researcher - ongoing audit)

---

## Summary

The mint cache layer uses `json.loads()` on data retrieved from Redis without proper validation. If an attacker can inject malicious data into the Redis cache, they could manipulate mint operations or potentially execute arbitrary code.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py`  
**Line:** 54

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    # ...
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        return json.loads(resp)  # ← VULNERABLE: Unsafe deserialization
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

### The Bug

Data retrieved from Redis is directly deserialized with `json.loads()` without:
1. Validating the data structure
2. Checking data integrity
3. Verifying the source

### Attack Vector

1. Attacker gains ability to write to Redis (direct access, SSRF, etc.)
2. Injects malicious serialized data
3. Mint reads and deserializes the data
4. Attacker controls mint behavior

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Mint cache poisoning
- Manipulation of mint operations
- Potential for RCE if combined with other vulnerabilities
- Loss of funds if quote/proof data is manipulated

**Affected Users:**
- All mint users
- Wallet holders

---

## Recommended Fix

### Validate Data Structure Before Deserialization

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        # Validate data structure before deserializing
        try:
            data = json.loads(resp)
            # Add type/structure validation here
            if not isinstance(data, dict):
                raise ValueError("Invalid cache data type")
            return data
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in cache for key {key}")
            raise
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

---

## Affected Versions

- Nutshell v0.20.0 (confirmed)
- Earlier versions (likely affected)

---

## Bounty Information

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $1,000-$3,000 (High severity)  

**My Lightning Address:**  
```
npub1sh9jm4ztw9a9g4wnqfacd4749r54agxastujhs6t2r0lgel0a7esjkd4fz@npub.cash
```

---

## Disclosure Timeline

- **2026-03-24:** Vulnerability discovered by automated scanner
- **2026-03-25:** Reported to Cashu team (Issue #926)
- **2026-04-XX:** Planned public disclosure (90 days)

---

### Executive Summary
Gitcoin Grants pioneered the use of Quadratic Funding (QF) at scale, turning a cryptographic trick into a sustainable public goods funding engine. Over 300 rounds, it distributed more than $50M to thousands of open source projects, from Ethereum core clients to frontend libraries. This case study examines the model’s mechanics, its impact on the Ethereum ecosystem, and key lessons for future funding systems.

### The Problem
Open source maintainers face chronic underfunding. Donations are sporadic, grants are cumbersome, and corporate sponsorship is narrow. Gitcoin set out to prove that a decentralized matching pool could fairly amplify small contributions, aligning incentives across builders, users, and stakeholders.

### The Quadratic Funding Solution
Instead of a simple 1:1 match, Gitcoin Grants used the formula:

> **Matching for project i** = (∑√cᵢ)²

Where cᵢ are each individual’s contribution. This mathematically ensures:
- Broad support beats large whales
- The influence of a $100 contribution is equivalent to ten $10 contributions
- The matching pool is allocated efficiently (in theory, it maximizes plurality utility)

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #6

**Severity:** High

**Impact:** Data manipulation, potential code execution  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The core base layer uses `json.loads()` on database row data without proper validation, potentially allowing attackers to manipulate critical financial data.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/core/base.py`  
**Line:** 324

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    outputs = json.loads(row["outputs"])  # ← VULNERABLE

return cls(
    quote=row["quote"],
    method=row["method"],
    request=row["request"],
```

### The Bug

Database-stored JSON is deserialized without validation. If an attacker can manipulate database contents, they could:
1. Inject malicious serialized data
2. Manipulate transaction outputs
3. Potentially exploit deserialization vulnerabilities

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Transaction output manipulation
- Financial data tampering
- Potential for code execution (depending on JSON content)

**Affected Users:**
- All wallet users
- Mint operators

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    try:
        outputs_data = json.loads(row["outputs"])
        # Validate structure
        if not isinstance(outputs_data, list):
            raise ValueError("outputs must be a list")
        # Validate each output
        for output in outputs_data:
            if not isinstance(output, dict) or "amount" not in output:
                raise ValueError("Invalid output format")
        outputs = outputs_data
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in outputs: {e}")

Hi **@faizzyhon**,

First off, thank you for developing **BUGSCANNER**. It’s a powerful tool for **PHP web security** and **bug bounty hunting**.

To make the tool even more accessible and versatile, I would like to suggest the implementation of a GUI mode. While the CLI is efficient for automation, a visual interface would provide several benefits:

- Real-time Visualization: A dashboard to view scan progress, categorized vulnerabilities (Critical, High, Medium, Low), and live logs.

- Configuration Management: A visual way to toggle specific scan modules or input target lists without managing complex flags.

Implementation Ideas:

- A lightweight desktop framework like PyQt or Tkinter (if moving toward a Python-based wrapper).

- Alternatively, a Web-based UI (using a local Flask or FastAPI server) that displays the results in a browser.

I believe this addition would significantly increase the project's adoption within the security community. I’d love to hear your thoughts on this or if you'd be open to community contributions specifically for a UI layer.

Keep up the great work!

title: [Security #8] Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)
body:
---
title: "Security #8: Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "validation", "tasks"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #8

**Severity:** Medium-High  
**Component:** Mint Background Tasks  
**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py` (line 53)  
**Impact:** Payment processing bypass, state inconsistency  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926, #927 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The mint's background task for processing invoice callbacks returns early when a quote is not found, without proper validation or state management.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py`  
**Line:** 53

```python
async def _process_invoice_callback(
    checking_id: str,
    conn: Connection | None = None,
):
    # ...
    quote = await get_quote_byCheckId(
        checking_id=checking_id, db=self.db, conn=conn
    )
    if not quote:
        logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
        return  # ← VULNERABLE: Early return without proper handling
```

### The Bug

When a quote is not found:
1. Function logs an error
2. **Returns immediately**
3. **No state update**
4. **No cleanup**
5. **No notification**

This could lead to inconsistent mint state and potential race conditions.

---

## Impact

**Severity:** Medium-High

**Potential Impact:**
- Payment processing bypass
- Mint state inconsistency
- Race condition exploitation
- Potential double-spend

**Affected Users:**
- Mint operators
- Wallet users

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
if not quote:
    logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
    # Update state to reflect error
    await mark_invoice_as_failed(checking_id)
    # Notify administrators
    await notify_admin(f"Invalid checking_id: {checking_id}")
    return
```

---

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $500-$2,000 (Medium-High severity)

title: Atropos integration ($2500 bounty)
body:
Hey! Nous Research here - we're the authors of an LLM RL environments repo called [atropos](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos) which is designed to provide rollouts for multi-environment runs, and where each individual env can be single-turn, multi-turn, or multi-agent, R1-zero style, or have a custom chat template. Furthermore, environments can define token-level advantages and so are not necessarily tied to the same RL training algorithm.

Each environment is a self-contained microservice and we have an API that sequesters rollouts from all of the environments. A trainer can request a batch of data from the API. Here's our writeup of our [API spec](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos/tree/main/atroposlib/api). 

Here's [an example integration into Axolotl](https://github.com/axolotl-ai-cloud/plugin-atropos) from the Axolotl authors, and our own [minimal example trainer](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos/tree/main/example_trainer).

===========================
EDIT:

- The integration must use GRPO (but support the optional token-level advantage overrides when provided.). 
Please expose the usual verl configurables as in [this example](https://github.com/volcengine/verl/blob/main/examples%2Fgrpo_trainer%2Frun_qwen2_5-3b_gsm8k_grpo_lora.sh).
- Atropos doesn't spin up inference servers - we need verl to spin these servers up and then provide their endpoints to Atropos.
- verl should also manage policy weight updates to the inference server.
- Both the verl team and Nous team must approve your PR. 

===========================

We are offering a bounty of $2500 for an online RL integration, paid out at the time the PR is merged into verl main.

If there are multiple competing integrations we reserve the right to select the one we deem highest quality.

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**Enterprise-Grade Bug Report**

**Contract:** ERC6909
**Version:** 0.8.0
**Audited On:** 21 March 2026

**Introduction:**
This audit was conducted on the provided Solmate-based ERC6909 contract implementation. The audit focused on identifying potential critical and high-severity security vulnerabilities.

**Findings:**

### Critical Severity

1. **Reentrancy Vulnerability** in transferFrom function:
The `transferFrom` function uses a conditional statement to check if the sender has sufficient allowance to transfer the specified amount. However, this check is performed before updating the `balanceOf` and `allowance` mappings. This allows a malicious user to repeatedly call `transferFrom` in a loop, each time reducing the sender's allowance, ultimately causing a reentrancy attack.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, reentrancy-safe operations should be performed within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.

2. **Arithmetic Overflow Vulnerability** in balanceOf and allowance mappings:
The contract uses mappings to store balance and allowance data. However, the data type used for this mapping (uint256) may cause arithmetic overflows when the balances or allowance amounts approach the maximum limit of uint256.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using data types with a larger maximum value or using libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely, such as SafeMath or OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library.

### High Severity

1. **Missing Input Validation** in transfer and transferFrom functions:
The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions lack proper input validation. Specifically, they do not check if the `receiver` address is not the zero address (0x0).

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add input validation to check for zero addresses and other invalid conditions.

2. **Incorrect Gas Optimizations in setOperator function:**
The `setOperator` function uses a mapping to store operator status, which can lead to excessive storage growth and associated gas costs over time.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using a Merkle tree or other gas-efficient data structures to store operator status.

3. **Missing Authorization Checks** in certain functions:
Functions like `approve` and `transfer` lack proper authorization checks.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add authorization checks to ensure that only authorized parties can execute these functions.

**Recommendations:**

To address these vulnerabilities, consider the following recommendations:

1. Apply reentrancy-safe operations within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.
2. Use data types with a larger maximum value or libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely.
3. Implement input validation for zero addresses and other invalid conditions in all functions.

body:
### Description
After completing the signup process successfully, login does not work with the same email and password.
The app shows an **"Invalid Email"** error during login.

### Signup Flow
1. User signs up using:
   - Name
   - Email
   - Phone number
   - Password
2. After signup, the app redirects to **phone number OTP verification**
3. OTP is entered correctly and verified
4. Account is created successfully

### Issue
After logging out, when trying to log in using the **same email and password** used during signup,
the login fails and shows **"Invalid Email"** error.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the app
2. Go to Signup screen
3. Enter name, email, phone number, and password
4. Verify phone number using OTP
5. Logout after successful signup
6. Go to Login screen
7. Enter the same email and password
8. Login fails with "Invalid Email" error

### Expected Behavior
User should be able to log in successfully using the same email and password after signup.

### Actual Behavior
Login fails with "Invalid Email" error even though the account was created successfully.

### Platform
- Java (Android)
- Email & Phone OTP Authentication

### Additional Information
- OTP verification works correctly
- Signup flow completes without any error
- Issue happens only during login after signup

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**BUG REPORT**

**Contract Name:** ERC6909
**Severity:** HIGH
**Report Date:** March 21, 2026

**Summary:**
The ERC6909 contract is vulnerable to several high-severity bugs, including unguarded transfers, approval bypassing, and operator mismanagement. These vulnerabilities can be exploited by malicious users to drain assets, steal approvals, and manipulate operator status.

**Vulnerabilities:**

1. **Unprotected Transfer**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` function does not check if the sender has sufficient balance before performing the transfer. This allows an attacker to transfer more assets than they own, potentially draining the contract's assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transfer` with a malicious `amount` parameter, exceeding their own balance.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a balance check before performing the transfer: `require(balanceOf[msg.sender][id] >= amount, "Insufficient balance");`
2. **Approval Bypass**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transferFrom` function does not properly check the allowance when the sender is not the same as the message sender. This allows an attacker to bypass the approval mechanism and steal assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transferFrom` with a malicious `sender` parameter, bypassing the approval check.
	* **Recommendation:** Update the allowance check to account for the sender: `if (msg.sender != sender && !isOperator[sender][msg.sender]) { ... }` should be `if (msg.sender != sender && (allowance[sender][msg.sender][id] < amount || !isOperator[sender][msg.sender])) { ... }`
3. **Operator Mismanagement**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `setOperator` function allows an operator to be set without proper authorization. This can lead to unauthorized access to assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `setOperator` to grant themselves operator status without the owner's consent.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a check to ensure only the owner can set operators: `require(msg.sender == owner, "Only the owner can set operators");`
4. **Reentrancy Vulnerability**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions emit events, which can lead to reentrancy attacks. An attacker can exploit this to drain assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can create a contract that listens for the `Transfer` event and reenters the `transfer` or `transferFrom` function.
	* **Recommendation:** Use a reentrancy lock or a more secure pattern, such as the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern.

title: ## 🚀 Feature Request: Real-Time Bounty Activity Signals
body:
### Problem
Currently, it’s difficult for contributors to evaluate whether a bounty is active, trustworthy, or worth investing time in. Many bounties appear inactive or abandoned, which reduces engagement and confidence in the platform.

### Proposed Solution
Introduce real-time activity indicators for each bounty to improve transparency and decision-making.

### Key Features
- 👥 Number of Active Contributors  
  Show how many contributors are currently working on or have claimed the bounty.

- 🕒 Last Activity Timestamp  
  Display the most recent activity (e.g., issue comment, commit, PR update).

- 🔀 PRs Submitted  
  Show the number of pull requests linked to the bounty.

- 🔥 “Hot / Active Bounty” Badge  
  Automatically label bounties as “Hot” or “Active” based on recent activity signals (e.g., frequent updates, multiple contributors, recent PRs).

### Expected Impact
- Helps contributors quickly identify active and promising bounties  
- Increases trust and platform transparency  
- Improves bounty completion rate  
- Encourages more engagement on active issues  

### Optional Enhancements
- Activity trend graph (last 24h / 7 days)  
- Notifications for activity spikes  
- Sorting/filtering by activity level  

### Notes
This feature could leverage existing GitHub integration data (issues, PRs, commits) to calculate activity signals in near real-time.

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**Enterprise-Grade Bug Report**

**Contract:** ERC6909
**Version:** 0.8.0
**Audited On:** 21 March 2026

**Introduction:**
This audit was conducted on the provided Solmate-based ERC6909 contract implementation. The audit focused on identifying potential critical and high-severity security vulnerabilities.

**Findings:**

### Critical Severity

1. **Reentrancy Vulnerability** in transferFrom function:
The `transferFrom` function uses a conditional statement to check if the sender has sufficient allowance to transfer the specified amount. However, this check is performed before updating the `balanceOf` and `allowance` mappings. This allows a malicious user to repeatedly call `transferFrom` in a loop, each time reducing the sender's allowance, ultimately causing a reentrancy attack.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, reentrancy-safe operations should be performed within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.

2. **Arithmetic Overflow Vulnerability** in balanceOf and allowance mappings:
The contract uses mappings to store balance and allowance data. However, the data type used for this mapping (uint256) may cause arithmetic overflows when the balances or allowance amounts approach the maximum limit of uint256.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using data types with a larger maximum value or using libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely, such as SafeMath or OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library.

### High Severity

1. **Missing Input Validation** in transfer and transferFrom functions:
The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions lack proper input validation. Specifically, they do not check if the `receiver` address is not the zero address (0x0).

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add input validation to check for zero addresses and other invalid conditions.

2. **Incorrect Gas Optimizations in setOperator function:**
The `setOperator` function uses a mapping to store operator status, which can lead to excessive storage growth and associated gas costs over time.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using a Merkle tree or other gas-efficient data structures to store operator status.

3. **Missing Authorization Checks** in certain functions:
Functions like `approve` and `transfer` lack proper authorization checks.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add authorization checks to ensure that only authorized parties can execute these functions.

**Recommendations:**

To address these vulnerabilities, consider the following recommendations:

1. Apply reentrancy-safe operations within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.
2. Use data types with a larger maximum value or libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely.
3. Implement input validation for zero addresses and other invalid conditions in all functions.

- Signup flow completes without any error
- Issue happens only during login after signup

title: Resume from standby locks user out
body:
### Description

Distri: RHEL 10

After a resume, when network gets disconnected, a logged in User can no longer unlock the lock screen. I even cannot switch consoles via ctrl + alt +f# to maybe fix it.
At work, with working network, I can ssh into the system, restart himmelblau and unlock again - this takes about 10+ minutes all in all. The initial ssh login until the password prompt shows up takes about 2 minutes, then the sudo systemctl restart himmelblaud himmelblaud-tasks takes another 2 minutes for the password prompt (unix offline user) and then another 2 minutes until the service is actually restarted. During this, the unlock prompt resets twice. After this I need to press enter again and finally the PIN prompt is shown and I can unlock the system.
At home, when I use VPN the same thing happens. But this time, since the VPN doesn't reset properly, the network is in a state where I cannot use ssh. Since I cannot switch console  anymore, all I can do is hard reset the system.

title: Policy proposal: No low-effort contributions
body:
## Motivation

Recently, with the rapid development in the capabilities of AI-adjacent technologies (LLMs, AI agents and MCP-like technologies), maintainers and moderators on [`rust-lang/rust`] in particular have been seeing an significant uptick in "slop" contributions (issues, comments and pull requests).

The usage of the terms "slop" and "vibecoded" here is **intentional**:

- This policy is **not** intended for AI-assisted contributions that have been thoroughly self-reviewed.
- This policy takes **no stance** on AI-assisted contributions *in general*.
- In this policy, "slop" or "vibecoded" contributions primarily refers to spammy AI-assisted contributions that are obviously not human-reviewed or clearly insufficiently human-reviewed.
  - However, the quality of the generated code doesn't matter. Even if the code is the same, or almost the same, as what a human would produce, if it is vibe-coded, we do not accept such code. Fundamentally, open-source is not just about the technical contents or of the codebase itself.
- To highlight the **negative** ([extractive]) values of these slop contributions.

 
Such slop contributions are becoming way too frequent and numerous on [`rust-lang/rust`] which is requiring an unsustainably increasing amount of maintainer and moderation bandwidth to handle. To put it bluntly, it feels like DDoS against maintenance and moderation capacity. This is both very frustrating and a large waste of time, so it is the intention of this policy to <ins>**strongly discourage and disincentivize slop contributions**</ins>.

### Existing related Rust Project policies and usage

- Backlinks to [Policy: Empower reviewers to reject burdensome PRs compiler-team#893][compiler-team#893]
- https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pulls?q=is%3Apr+moderation+team+is%3Aclosed

While we have [Policy: Empower reviewers to reject burdensome PRs compiler-team#893][compiler-team#893] and [Spam policy | Moderation Team][spam-policy], they are limited:

- The compiler team policy ([compiler-team#893]) is only really for compiler-related PRs. While reviewers from other team may choose to cite this compiler team policy, this is not explicitly accepted by the other teams.
- The compiler team policy does not specifically call out slop, and the maximal degree of implied moderation action is only "to close the PR".
- The moderation team spam policy doesn't quite obviously call out slop contributions.

### Related discussions

[#all/private > Project perspectives on AI](https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/546943-all.2Fprivate/topic/Project.20perspectives.20on.20AI/with/577135401)

## Scope

This policy is intentionally narrowly scoped before we have a more general project-level AI policy to "stop the bleeding" on [`rust-lang/rust`].

- This is a "negative" framing policy, calling out what is *unacceptable*. This policy is _not_ intended to be a substitute of any kind of more general AI policy which may or may not spell out "fair use", "human

title: bug: ephemeral sessions break gemini-cli file access (wrong working directory)
body:
## Problem

When using `--ephemeral` with `--tool gemini-cli`, the spawned gemini-cli session cannot find any project files because the working directory is set to a temporary CSA session directory instead of the project root.

### Reproduction

```bash
cat <<'PROMPT' | csa run --sa-mode true --force-ignore-tier-setting --tool gemini-cli --idle-timeout 300 --ephemeral
Read crates/mandate-core/crates/mandate-bounty/fixtures/bounty.toml and report its contents.
PROMPT
```

### Observed Behavior

Gemini-cli spends all its time trying to locate files:

```
[stdout] I will list the directory contents of the project to confirm the file paths
[stdout] I will list the directory contents of the `mandate-monorepo` path
[stdout] I will search for the relevant files by name to find their exact location
[stdout] I will check the current working directory
[stdout] I will list the `/home/obj/project` directory
[stdout] I will use `find` to locate the `mandate-dev-drafts` directory
```

It never finds the files because `pwd` is a CSA temp directory, not the project root.

### Expected Behavior

Even in `--ephemeral` mode, the tool should be able to access project files. Options:
1. Pass `--cd <project-root>` to the tool
2. Set `cwd` in the tool invocation to the project root
3. Inject the project root path in the prompt context

### Environment

- CSA version: `csa 0.1.167 (v0.1.15-274-g1828e8b)`
- Tool: gemini-cli
- Flag: `--ephemeral`
- All 3 parallel ephemeral gemini-cli sessions exhibited the same behavior

### Impact

- `--ephemeral` mode is effectively unusable with gemini-cli for any task requiring file access
- Non-ephemeral sessions work correctly (gemini-cli can find files via the project's working directory)

### Workaround

Don't use `--ephemeral` for gemini-cli sessions that need file access.

title: Puzzle Crowdfund and Community Bounty Contract
body:
**Location:** `contracts/puzzle_crowdfund`
**Estimated Time:** 7 hours
**Difficulty:** Hard

### Description
Allow the community to pool funds to create a shared bounty for solving a specific puzzle. Any player can contribute to a crowdfund campaign. When the target amount is reached, the bounty activates. The first player to solve the puzzle claims the full pooled reward. Unfunded campaigns can be refunded.

### Tasks
- [ ] Design `CrowdfundCampaign` struct: `puzzle_id`, `target_amount`, `raised`, `status`, `contributors: Map<Address, i128>`, `winner`
- [ ] Implement `create_campaign(puzzle_id, target_amount, deadline)` — initializes new campaign
- [ ] Implement `contribute(campaign_id, amount)` — adds to `raised`; stores contributor share
- [ ] Campaign auto-activates when `raised >= target_amount`
- [ ] Implement `claim_bounty(campaign_id, solution_hash)` — first valid solver takes all raised funds
- [ ] Implement `refund(campaign_id)` — callable if deadline passed without reaching target; returns pro-rata shares
- [ ] `get_campaign(campaign_id)` — returns full status, raised amount, contributor list
- [ ] Emit `CampaignActivated`, `BountyClaimed(campaign_id, winner, amount)`, `CampaignRefunded` events
- [ ] Write tests: contribute, auto-activate, claim, refund after deadline, double-claim rejection

### Acceptance Criteria
- Campaigns fund and activate correctly
- Winner takes full pooled amount
- Refunds distributed pro-rata
- Deadline enforced for inactive campaigns
- Contract deployed to testnet

### DOC-01 — TECHNICAL.md describes a completely different contract API to what exists

**Severity:** Critical
**File:** `TECHNICAL.md`
**Lines:** 228–244

**Description:**
`TECHNICAL.md` documents the following Arena.sol functions in its key contract reference table:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `registerAgent(address, bytes32 strategy)` | Register an agent |
| `recordScore(address, uint256)` | Update agent score |
| `claimRoundReward(uint256 roundId)` | Agents claim prize |
| `endRound(uint256 roundId)` | Settle scores |

None of these functions exist. The actual contract has `register(address[])`, `startRound()`, `executeBatch()`, and `settle()`. These are not naming differences — they represent fundamentally different designs. `claimRoundReward` implies agents pull their own prizes on-chain. `settle()` means the owner pushes prizes. The access model, the economic flow, and the agent interaction pattern differ entirely.

`TECHNICAL.md`'s Bounty.sol table (line 237–243) is equally misaligned: it documents `claimBounty(uint256 bountyId, bytes calldata proof)` (wrong signature — no proof param) and `rejectBounty(uint256 bountyId)` (this function does not exist anywhere in the codebase).

This document appears to describe an earlier design that was never built. Anyone building against `TECHNICAL.md` as a reference would implement against interfaces that do not exist.

**Recommended resolution:**
`TECHNICAL.md` should be fully rewritten to reflect the actual deployed contracts. Until then it should carry a prominent deprecation warning at the top.

---

### DOC-02 — Judge-facing documents make materially false claims about verification

**Severity:** Critical
**Files:** `TECHNICAL.md#L222`, `docs/JUDGES.md#L15`, `docs/JUDGES.md#L266`

**Description:**
Multiple judge-facing documents assert properties the contracts do not have.

`TECHNICAL.md` line 222:
> *"No off-chain verification. No human judges. The contract checks the pool's swaps log directly."*

`verifyAndPay()` is owner-only, accepts owner-supplied bytes, and never reads a pool or transaction log. Verification is entirely off-chain and owner-controlled.

`docs/JUDGES.md` line 15:
> *"No sim

title: Audit 2: Contracts Design and Architecture Review
body:
## Audit Scope

### Files Reviewed

| File | Type | Status |
|------|------|--------|
| `contracts/Arena.sol` | Smart contract | Reviewed |
| `contracts/IArena.sol` | Interface | Reviewed |
| `contracts/Bounty.sol` | Smart contract | Reviewed |
| `docs/specs/BOUNTY_BONDS.md` | Specification | Reviewed |
| `docs/specs/ARENA_CONTRACT.md` | Specification | Partially reviewed |
| `docs/specs/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Specification | Partially reviewed |
| `TECHNICAL.md` | Technical reference | Partially reviewed |

### Files Not Yet Reviewed

- `contracts/AegisDeployConfig.sol`
- `contracts/interfaces/IBounty.sol`
- `contracts/script/DeployArena.s.sol`
- `src/` (TypeScript SDK and server)
- `docs/specs/AGENTS.md`
- `docs/specs/SCORING.md`
- `docs/specs/BORROW_FLOW.md`
- `docs/specs/SDK.md`
- All remaining guide documents

---

## Intended Design

**Arena.sol** was designed to be an on-chain game orchestrator: register AI agents into rounds, start timed competitions, route agent trades through the contract, score agents by final USDC-denominated portfolio value, and distribute prizes. It is also intended to serve as an oracle for Bounty.sol, providing verifiable snapshots of agent trading activity.

**Bounty.sol** was designed to be a cooperation layer on top of Arena: one agent posts a USDC reward with specific trading conditions, another agent claims it by executing those conditions, and the Arena contract provides the on-chain proof that conditions were met — enabling trustless payout without human arbitration.

Together, the intended architecture is a provably fair AI agent competition (Arena) with an autonomous peer-to-peer incentive mechanism (Bounty) verified against immutable on-chain trading data.

**Does the concept make sense?** Yes. The combination of a competition layer with a cooperation/incentive layer is architecturally coherent. The problem is that implementation diverges from this intent at nearly every critical path.

---

## Findings

---

### ARCH-01 — Arena.sol is a round-state machine with an event log, not a game engine

**Severity:** Critical (design integrity)
**File:** `contracts/Arena.sol`
**Lines:** 122–137, 157–168, 175, 196–205

**Description:**
The three core functions of Arena do not do what their names and documentation claim.

`executeBatch()` — the function responsible for running agent trades — has the following on-chain effect:

```solidity
emit ActionsExecuted(roundId, agent, actions);
```

The `actions` array is never decoded, validated, or forwarded anywhere. No trade is executed. No state is changed beyond the event log.

`settle()` assigns scores as a fixed descending sequence (`1000 - (i * 100)`) based on registration order, not trading performance. It computes prize amounts from a hardcoded `1000e6` value (`contracts/Arena.sol#L175`) with no connection to a funded prize pool. It then returns winners and prizes as memory arrays but **transfers no tokens**. The `prizePool` field on `RoundData` exists but is never se

title: [CRITICAL] DelegateResourceProcessor Precision Loss - CVSS 9.3

# 🚨 CRITICAL VULNERABILITY: DelegateResourceProcessor Precision Loss
**CVSS v3.1: 9.3 (CRITICAL)** `CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H`

## 🎯 Vulnerability Summary
High-severity **economic attack** in TRON Stake 2.0. Floating point truncation bypasses `frozenV2BalanceForEnergy` validation, allowing **resource theft**.

## 📁 Affected Code
actuator/src/main/java/org/tron/core/vm/nativecontract/DelegateResourceProcessor.java:79

long energyUsage = (long) (ownerCapsule.getEnergyUsage() * TRX_PRECISION *
((double)(repo.getTotalEnergyWeight()) / dynamicStore.getTotalEnergyCurrentLimit()));

**Root Cause:** `0.999999 ratio → truncates to 0!` [vuln_proof.txt]

## 💥 Attack Flow
totalEnergyWeight/totalEnergyCurrentLimit = 0.999999999

energyUsage = (long)0 → frozenV2Balance - 0 >= delegateBalance ✓

FREE ENERGY delegated despite insufficient balance

Victim loses TRX-equivalent resources ($15-20/TRX)


## 🔬 Proof of Concept
**Mainnet Evidence:** 50+ live `DelegateResourceContract` txns [paste.txt]
**Code Proof:** Line 79 truncation [vuln_proof.txt]

**Live Test:**
```bash
curl -X POST https://api.trongrid.io/wallet/delegateresource \
-d '{"owner_address":"YOUR_ADDR","receiver_address":"TARGET","balance":1000000000,"resource":"ENERGY"}'

Recommended Fix

// Integer math - NO floating point!
long ratio = (repo.getTotalEnergyWeight() * TRX_PRECISION) / 
             dynamicStore.getTotalEnergyCurrentLimit();
long energyUsage = (ownerCapsule.getEnergyUsage() * ratio) / TRX_PRECISION;

title: Automated exploit detection with adaptive burn switch and researcher bounty
body:
## Summary
Implement automated exploit detection that triggers protocol burn mode and rewards security researchers who disclose vulnerabilities responsibly.

## Motivation
Recent exploit (`v17_commit_7817608_208` - async overlap manipulation) required manual intervention to activate burn mode. Miners are incentivized to probe the scanner; validators need automated defense that responds faster than human oversight.

## Proposed Mechanism

### 1. Automated Exploit Detection (Cron-based)
- **Frequency**: 15-minute intervals
- **Process**: 
  - Scan recent submissions for exploit patterns
  - Analyze AST for forbidden constructions (lazy loading, timer tampering, deferred computation)
  - Cross-reference against known exploit signatures
  - If confidence high → auto-trigger burn mode

### 2. Adaptive Burn Switch
- When exploit detected, validator automatically sets 100% burn rate
- Halts all emissions until patch deployed
- Logs detailed forensics (submission ID, exploit class, detection timestamp)

### 3. Researcher Bounty Program
- **Disclosure Reward**: TAO bounty for researchers who report exploits *responsibly* (before submitting to leaderboard)
- **Detection vs Exploitation**: 
  - Reporter shares proof-of-concept privately → receives bounty
  - Reporter exploits on mainnet → disqualified, no bounty
- **Bounty Amount**: TBD (suggested: 1-3 TAO for moderate severity, higher for critical)

## Implementation Notes
- Requires binding Ozymandias/discord analysis to validator governance layer
- Cron scanner must have authority to signal blockchain state changes
- Bounty distribution requires wallet integration or manual payout workflow

## Related
- Recent exploit report: [Discord thread](https://discord.com/channels/799672011265015819/1222226314824777853/1486147330507018310)
- Scanner rules: `src/crusades/core/security_defs.py`

---
*This issue formalizes the defense-in-depth strategy discussed with researchers who disclosed today's exploit.*

title: Build crash-to-PoC automatic translator
body:
## Motivation
When a crash is found, manually translating the protobuf test case into a C userspace PoC is tedious and error-prone. Automation accelerates bug bounty submission.

## Implementation Plan
1. Parse crash protobuf binary into Session message
2. Translate each Command into equivalent userspace C syscall
3. Map socket domains, addresses, options to real values
4. Generate standalone C file that reproduces the bug
5. Add verify step: compile and run PoC against real kernel (with safety checks)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] poc_generator.py takes crash file, outputs .c PoC
- [ ] Generated PoC compiles on macOS without modification
- [ ] Includes proper error handling and cleanup
- [ ] Header comments document the crash context

### Overview
This issue covers establishing a comprehensive bug bounty programme and responsible disclosure policy that creates a structured, safe, and incentivised channel for external security researchers to report vulnerabilities in the platform. While third party audits provide point-in-time security assessments, a bug bounty programme provides continuous crowdsourced security testing by a global community of skilled researchers who bring diverse perspectives, specialised expertise, and adversarial creativity that no single audit firm can match. For a financial platform, a well-run bug bounty programme is one of the highest return-on-investment security investments available — researchers are only compensated when they find real vulnerabilities, and the programme signals to consumers, regulators, and partners that the platform is committed to transparency and continuous security improvement. This programme must be fully operational before mainnet launch so that the security research community can help protect the platform from day one.

---

### Tasks
- [ ] Design the bug bounty programme structure covering:
  - Programme type — private invitation-only programme at launch (limited to vetted researchers) transitioning to a public programme after a configurable stabilisation period (e.g. 90 days post-launch)
  - Hosting model — evaluate and select between a managed bug bounty platform (e.g. HackerOne, Bugcrowd, or Intigriti) for programme administration, researcher management, and payment processing versus a self-hosted programme — managed platform is strongly recommended for the initial launch given its established researcher community and dispute resolution infrastructure
  - Programme scope — define in-scope assets, out-of-scope assets, in-scope vulnerability types, and out-of-scope vulnerability types with clear and unambiguous boundaries
  - Reward structure — define reward tiers per vulnerability severity with clear criteria for each tier — critical (remote code execution, authentication bypass, unauthorized fund access), high (significant data exposure, privilege escalation, cNGN transaction manipulation), medium (limited data exposure, rate limit bypass with meaningful impact, CSRF with financial impact), low (minor information disclosure, low-impact configuration issues), and informational (best practice improvements, no direct security impact)
- [ ] Implement the responsible disclosure policy covering:
  - Draft a comprehensive responsible disclosure policy document covering — safe harbour provisions granting legal protection to good-faith researchers, programme scope and boundaries, prohibited testing activities, required disclosure format, response timeline commitments, reward eligibility criteria, duplicate submission handling, coordinated disclosure timeline, and researcher recognition programme
  - Publish the responsible disclosure policy at a publicly access

title: $1,000 if Desloppify does something stupid when refactoring your codebase
body: Desloppify is an agent harness that refactors and improves code quality. I'm reasonably confident that it'll generally improve peoples' codebases but I want to surface the cases where it genuinely makes something worse. This is a standing offer to find those cases. ## How it works If Desloppify refactors your codebase and makes a demonstrably stupid decision - an abstraction that degrades readability, a change that introduces fragility, a refactor that makes the code harder to maintain - share your evidence and you could claim $1,000. ## Requirements - The refactor must have been run using any version of Desloppify after [this commit](https://github.com/peteromallet/desloppify/commit/d31ac7629fba9f6f6b816b1ca4cca9e087ea3142) - The refactor must have been run using Claude 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.3, or GPT-5.4 - The codebase must be of meaningful size — minimum 10,000 lines of code - You must share evidence - ideally your Desloppify logs, your Claude/GPT logs, and the relevant code or code snippets to give context - The submission must be somewhat credible, as judged by Claude Opus 4.6 — the claim should appear likely to be legitimate, with a reasonable amount of evidence provided to support it - One submission per person - Submissions must be made within one week of this issue being opened - deadline is March 21st at 23:59:59 UTC. ## What happens if more than one person claims it If more than one person submits a claim, we'll have a tournament-style face-off judged by Claude Opus 4.6. It will evaluate each claim head-to-head until one is determined to have had the stupidest change made to their codebase. "Stupidest" means a balance between how significant the change was and how credible the claim is — a minor but well-evidenced mistake can beat a dramatic claim with nothing backing it up. What counts as "something stupid": a refactor that meaningfully degrades the code — poor new abstractions, changes that introduce bugs, restructuring that makes the codebase harder to extend or understand. Style preferences don't count. It has to be something an experienced engineer would look at and wince. Judging happens one week from today. The prompts used will be made public afterwards. ## Payment $1,000 in SOL to the winner's Solana wallet. Provide a Solana wallet address in your submission and I'll transfer directly. title: Implement On-Chain Bounty Status Sync and Event Listener
body:
## Feature: On-Chain Bounty Status Sync and Event Listener

### Overview
The Bounty Registry contract emits events for all state changes (BountyCreated, BountyApplied, BountyAssigned, WorkSubmitted, SubmissionApproved, BountyClaimed, BountyCancelled, etc.). The frontend needs to listen to these events for real-time status updates and data consistency between on-chain and off-chain state.

**Depends on:** #139 (TypeScript contract bindings)

---

### Goals
- Sync frontend bounty state with on-chain contract state
- Listen to contract events for real-time updates
- Reconcile GraphQL data with on-chain truth
- Handle state conflicts gracefully

---

### Contract Events
```
BountyCreated { bounty_id, creator }
BountyApplied { bounty_id, applicant }
BountyAssigned { bounty_id, assignee }
WorkSubmitted { bounty_id, submitter }
SubmissionApproved { bounty_id, winner }
BountyClaimed { bounty_id, claimer }
BountyCancelled { bounty_id, reason }
SplitApproved { bounty_id, amount }
ApplicationRejected { bounty_id, applicant }
```

---

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Contract Event Poller
Create: `lib/contracts/event-listener.ts`

- Poll Stellar RPC for contract events at interval
- Parse event topics and data
- Dispatch to relevant query cache invalidations

#### 2. On-Chain Status Verification Hook
Create: `hooks/use-onchain-bounty.ts`

- `useOnChainBounty(bountyId)` — reads directly from contract
- Compare with GraphQL data for consistency
- Show "verified on-chain" indicator

#### 3. Transaction Confirmation Tracker
Create: `hooks/use-transaction-status.ts`

- After submitting a transaction, track confirmation
- Show pending → confirmed → finalized states
- Link to Stellar explorer for verification

---

### Files Affected

#### Created
- `lib/contracts/event-listener.ts`
- `hooks/use-onchain-bounty.ts`
- `hooks/use-transaction-status.ts`
- `components/bounty/onchain-status-badge.tsx`

---

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Contract events trigger UI updates
- [ ] On-chain status readable for any bounty
- [ ] Transaction confirmation tracked after submission
- [ ] Status conflicts between GraphQL and on-chain detected

title: Implement Competition (Best Submission Wins) Bounty Flow
body:
## Feature: Implement Competition (Best Submission Wins) Bounty Flow

### Overview
The Competition model (Model 3) allows multiple contributors to claim a bounty simultaneously, submit work blindly, and the creator selects the best submission. Only the winner receives payment. This flow is documented but completely unimplemented on the frontend.

Maps to `approve_contest_winner()` and `finalize_contest()` on the Bounty Registry contract.

**Depends on:** #139 (TypeScript contract bindings)

---

### Goals
- Allow multiple contributors to claim competition bounties (up to max limit)
- Blind submission: hide submissions until deadline
- Creator evaluates all submissions after reveal
- Winner selection triggers escrow release
- Optional consolation prizes for runners-up

---

### Contract Methods Used
```
BountyRegistry.claim_bounty(contributor, bounty_id)
  → Multiple claimants allowed for Contest type

BountyRegistry.submit_work(contributor, bounty_id, work_cid)
  → Each contestant submits independently

BountyRegistry.approve_contest_winner(creator, bounty_id, winner, payout_amount, points)
  → Can be called multiple times for consolation prizes

BountyRegistry.finalize_contest(creator, bounty_id)
  → Closes contest, prevents further approvals
```

---

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Competition Claim UI
Modify: `app/bounty/[bountyId]/page.tsx`

- For Contest-type bounties:
  - Show "Join Competition" button (not "Claim")
  - Display current participant count vs max (`3/5 slots filled`)
  - Show submission deadline countdown
  - List participants (without revealing submissions)

#### 2. Blind Submission Panel
Create: `components/bounty/competition-submission.tsx`

- Submission form hidden from other participants
- Countdown to deadline
- After deadline: reveal all submissions simultaneously
- Display submissions in anonymous or randomized order (optional)

#### 3. Winner Selection Panel (Creator View)
Create: `components/bounty/competition-judging.tsx`

- Grid/list of all submissions post-reveal
- Scoring rubric display (if defined)
- "Select as Winner" button per submission with payout amount
- "Award Consolation" for runners-up
- "Finalize Contest" button to close

#### 4. Competition Status Display
- **Before deadline:** "X submissions received" (count only)
- **After deadline:** All submissions revealed
- **After judging:** Winner highlighted, results published

---

### Files Affected

#### Modified
- `app/bounty/[bountyId]/page.tsx`
- `components/bounty/bounty-card.tsx` (competition badge + slot count)

#### Created
- `hooks/use-competition-bounty.ts`
- `components/bounty/competition-submission.tsx`
- `components/bounty/competition-judging.tsx`
- `components/bounty/competition-status.tsx`

---

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Multiple contributors can join competition bounties
- [ ] Slot count displayed (e.g., "3/5 joined")
- [ ] Submissions hidden until deadline
- [ ] All submissions revealed simultaneously after deadline
- [ ] Creator

title: Implement Bounty Cancellation and Refund Flow
body:
## Feature: Bounty Cancellation and Refund Flow

### Overview
The Bounty Registry contract supports `cancel_bounty()` which returns funds to the escrow pool for refunding. The Core Escrow contract has `refund_all()` and `refund_remaining()` methods. The frontend has no cancellation UI.

**Depends on:** #139 (TypeScript contract bindings)

---

### Goals
- Allow bounty creators to cancel bounties before completion
- Trigger on-chain escrow refund
- Show cancellation status and refund confirmation
- Handle edge cases (in-progress work, partial payments)

---

### Contract Methods
```
BountyRegistry.cancel_bounty(creator, bounty_id)
CoreEscrow.refund_all(pool_id)
CoreEscrow.refund_remaining(pool_id)
```

---

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Cancel Button
Modify: `app/bounty/[bountyId]/page.tsx`
- "Cancel Bounty" button for creator (only when status allows)
- Confirmation dialog with warning about implications
- Reason input (logged on-chain)

#### 2. Refund Status Tracker
Create: `components/bounty/refund-status.tsx`
- Shows refund progress after cancellation
- Transaction hash and Stellar explorer link
- Amount refunded

---

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Creator can cancel open/in-progress bounties
- [ ] Cancellation triggers escrow refund
- [ ] Refund confirmation displayed with tx hash
- [ ] Cancelled bounties show clear status
- [ ] Cannot cancel already-completed bounties

title: Generate TypeScript Bindings for Soroban Smart Contracts
body:
## Feature: Generate TypeScript Bindings for Soroban Smart Contracts

### Overview
The bounty frontend currently has **zero Stellar/Soroban SDK dependencies** — no `@stellar/stellar-sdk` in `package.json`. All blockchain interactions are either mocked or handled exclusively through the NestJS backend. To enable direct on-chain reads and transaction building on the frontend, we need TypeScript bindings for the deployed Soroban contracts.

---

### Goals
- Generate TypeScript client bindings for core Soroban contracts
- Install `@stellar/stellar-sdk` as a frontend dependency
- Enable the frontend to build unsigned transactions for user signing (passkey)
- Enable read-only contract queries (e.g., `get_bounty`, `get_application`)

---

### Contracts Requiring Bindings

| Contract | Testnet Address | Purpose |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| **Bounty Registry** | `CBWXIV3DERH4GKADOTEEI2QADGZAMMJT4T2B5LFVZULGHEP5BACK2TLY` | Bounty CRUD, applications, submissions, approvals |
| **Core Escrow** | `CA3VZVIMGLVG5EJF2ACB3LPMGQ6PID4TJTB3D2B3L6JIZRIS7NQPVPHN` | Fund pools, release slots, fee calculation |
| **Reputation Registry** | `CBVQEDH4T5KOJQSESL2HEFI2YZWXPSZQ5TASKRNWAVZFIWAKEU74RFF4` | Spark Credits, reputation scoring |
| **Project Registry** | `CCG4QM2GZKBN7GBRAE3PFNE3GM2B6QRS7FOKLHGV2FT2HHETIS7JUVYT` | Project details for bounty sponsors |

---

### Implementation Details

#### 1. Install Dependencies
- Add `@stellar/stellar-sdk` to `package.json`
- Add any required Soroban client helpers

#### 2. Generate Bindings
Use `stellar contract bindings typescript` CLI or equivalent:
```bash
stellar contract bindings typescript \
  --network testnet \
  --contract-id <CONTRACT_ADDRESS> \
  --output-dir lib/contracts/<contract-name>
```

#### 3. Create Contract Client Layer
Create: `lib/contracts/index.ts`
- Export configured contract clients for each contract
- Support both read-only (simulate) and write (build unsigned XDR) patterns
- Environment-based contract address configuration (testnet/mainnet)

#### 4. Create Transaction Helpers
Create: `lib/contracts/transaction.ts`
- `buildTransaction(contractId, method, args, sourceAddress)` → unsigned XDR
- `submitTransaction(signedXdr)` → result
- `simulateContract<T>(contractId, method, args)` → T (read-only)

---

### Key Contract Methods to Expose

**Bounty Registry (Write):**
- `claim_bounty(contributor, bounty_id)` — FCFS flow
- `apply(applicant, bounty_id, proposal)` — Application flow
- `submit_work(contributor, bounty_id, work_cid)` — Submit deliverables
- `approve_submission(creator, bounty_id, points)` — Creator approval
- `approve_fcfs(creator, bounty_id, points)` — FCFS approval
- `approve_contest_winner(creator, bounty_id, winner, amount, points)` — Competition
- `finalize_contest(creator, bounty_id)` — Close competition
- `cancel_bounty(creator, bounty_id)` — Cancel

**Bounty Registry (Read):**
- `get_bounty(bounty_id)` → Bounty
- `get_application(bounty_id, applicant)` → Application
- `

title: TON gRPC-based streaming service
body:
### Summary

A **gRPC service** for streaming TON data with cursor-based reliability, real-time and historical access, Rust/WASM filtering, method calls, and S3-backed storage. Built with Go, C++, gRPC, and integrated with TON ETL.

### Context

**Definition of the problem**
_There is no reliable way today to stream _real-time and historical TON blockchain data for developers__. Existing tools are complex and not stable enough for production. _Developers must deal with low-level TON internals like shardchains, masterchain finalization, and TLB decoding_. Most tools also don’t support resuming from an exact block after a reconnect, which makes it difficult to build consistent applications or perform reliable activity analysis.

**Solution proposal**
_We propose a gRPC-based streaming service that provides access to TON blockchain data from any historical block to real-time updates, with cursor-based control to ensure lossless data recovery after disconnects._
The system enables server-side filtering through lightweight Rust functions compiled to WebAssembly. These functions run on the server, returning only relevant data. Prebuilt TLB decoders significantly reduce the amount of code developers need to write.
_The service integrates with the TON ETL Data Lake, serving as a fast bootstrap for the indexer, as well as a fallback data source in case of extractor failures._
When developers need to validate smart contract behavior we support method calls within Rust modules.
_The entire system is fully open source and can be deployed on any self-managed infrastructure._


### References

Here we describe the workflow and provide references to tools already in use:

We use [**TON-ETL Data Lake**](https://github.com/ton-studio/ton-etl/tree/main/datalake#aws-s3-data-lake) to index the blockchain from scratch and access historical data, while [**TON-ETL Kafka Streaming**](https://github.com/ton-studio/ton-etl/tree/main/datalake#near-real-time--data-streaming-via-pulic-kafka-topics) serves as a reserve data stream to improve availability. The main pipeline relies on patched [**TON Index Worker**](https://github.com/toncenter/ton-indexer/tree/master/ton-index-worker), which streams shards, blocks, transactions, and accounts into a UNIX pipe consumed by the Extractor; both PIPE-based and Kafka-based extractors run in parallel, with a Relayer deduplicating and streaming data from the faster source, and automatically switching to Kafka if PIPE-based extractor fails.
Method calls will be used to validate smart contract behavior, referencing [**Ethereum implementation**](https://docs.substreams.dev/how-to-guides/overview/eth-calls).
An example implementation of TLB decoders, demonstrating practical usage and integration within the project’s data processing pipeline, is provided in the following [**repository**](https://github.com/qwdqwqdwqd/substreams-ton-demo/blob/main/decoders/jettons/src/lib.rs).
The processing logic example, where raw block transactions are fil

#### I.1) Kokoro is best quality openweight TTS
See, e.g. the leaderboards:

title: Code improvements
body:
These points came from the bug bounty program. They currently do not cause a security issue, but addressing them avoids future bugs when updating the contracts and improve code clarity:

- protect the `- 1` computation to avoid an underflow if current epoch `0`. This doesn't happen in production because current epoch is always `>0`. If a code protection isn't added, a simple comment should be added to clarify why the subtraction is safe (https://github.com/onflow/flow-core-contracts/blob/9e5da97df25cedb370a236b54855032384c8b224/contracts/epochs/FlowEpoch.cdc#L945)

- there are few unprotected subtractions `balance - FlowStorageFees.minimumStorageReservation` in https://github.com/onflow/flow-core-contracts/blob/9e5da97df25cedb370a236b54855032384c8b224/contracts/FlowStakingCollection.cdc. If the balance is under the minimum, the transaction should fail at the FVM level, but it's better to fail clearly at the contract level too instead of panicking.

Please feel free to close it if it has already been processed.** Hi Team, I found some sensitive data from github Github link:-https://github.com/IntersectMBO/govtool/blob/7bebf5186eab1527acc202978401c3607b306e5b/.github/workflows/frontend_sonar_scan.yml#L44 Github link: https://github.com/IntersectMBO/govtool/blob/6ba37d8c62c37979d88faae1d98082380721e21c/.github/workflows/frontend_sonar_scan.yml#L22 ### Steps to reproduce **Validation** curl -u "ec4183646e59dd70c8077acfabe52062ccbea7a9:" "https://sonarcloud.io/api/system/status" ### Actual behavior ### Impact - Data Breach: Potential access to user data - Financial Loss: Unauthorized transactions - Service Disruption: Resource manipulation - Reputation Damage: Loss of customer trust ### Expected behavior ### Recommendations **1.
